'''Kotai Jingū''' (皇大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Imafuku Minami|ja|今福南|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imafuku Minami|1=Imafuku-minami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q76307293}}, {{ill|Jōtō-ku, Osaka|en|Jōtō-ku, Osaka|WD=Q490508|lt=Joto Ward|1=Jōtō-ku, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka City|1=Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is affectionately known as "Imafuku Ebisu".
== History ==
The founding year is unknown<ref name="Osaka City">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/joto/page/0000000740.html |title=皇大神宮 |publisher=Osaka City |accessdate=2024-08-15 |language=ja}}</ref>. It is said to have been established during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} when {{ill|Kuki Imafuku Mikuriya|ja|久岐今福御厨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Imafuku Mikuriya|qq=}} was developed<ref name="Osaka City" />. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} edict, it was known as the {{ill|Thirty Guardian Deities Shrine|ja|三十番神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thirty Guardian Deities Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Under the modern shrine Ranking system, it was designated a village shrine, received accounting designation in 1911 ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 44), and became a {{ill|Shinsen heihaku ryō kyōshinsha|ja|神饌幣帛供進社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen heihaku ryō kyōshinsha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 1912. In 1947 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 22), it was removed from state control under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|WD=Q3029647|lt=Shintō Directive|1=Shinto Directive}}<ref name="Official">{{Cite web |url=https://koutaijingu.wixsite.com/mysite/blank-1 |title=由緒 |publisher=皇大神宮 |accessdate=2024-11-20 |language=ja}}</ref>.
The current shrine building was renovated in 1742 ({{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}} 2), 1842 ({{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō era|1=Tenpō}} 13), and 1880 (Meiji 13), and reconstructed by {{ill|Furukawa Shigeharu|ja|古川重春|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furukawa Shigeharu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who designed {{ill|Osaka Castle|ja|大阪城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂城|1=Osaka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q321242}}, in 1932 (Shōwa 7)<ref name="Osaka City" /><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.osaka-jinjacho.jp/funai_jinja/dai4shibu/joto-ku/04006kotaijingu.html |title=皇大神宮 |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2024-08-15}}</ref>.
In the {{ill|Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|ja|阪神・淡路大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|1=Damage by Great Hanshin earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392882}} of 1995 (Heisei 7), part of the worship hall collapsed. It was repaired the following year with a copper roof replacing the previous tile roof.
The {{ill|Kinoshita Yōemon Land Survey Record|ja|木下与右衛門御検地帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinoshita Yōemon Land Survey Record|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1594, Bunroku 3) states that the shrine was exempted from taxation on an area measuring 26 ken east to west and 17 ken north to south (1 tan, 4 se, 4 chōbu)<ref name="Official" />{{Sfn|Kimura|1971|p={{page needed|date=November 2024}}}}.
The stone guidepost at the southwest corner of the worship hall was erected in March 1833 (Tenpō 3) by six caretakers. Originally, it stood at the southeastern corner of the crossroads on the {{ill|Kotsutsumi Kaidō (Old Embankment Road)|ja|古堤街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotsutsumi Kaidō (Old Embankment Road)|1=Q11411014|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11411014}} (Yamato Road), a major route for pilgrimages to {{ill|Nozaki|ja|野崎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozaki|1=Q11645363|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645363}}, {{ill|Mount Ōmine|en|Mount Ōmine|WD=Q3695989|lt=Ōmine Mountain|1=Mount Ōmine}}, and {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. A local legend recounts that villagers danced wildly here during the {{ill|Okagemairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okagemairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} pilgrimage frenzy, and the guidepost was later moved to the shrine grounds{{Sfn|Kimura|1971|p={{page needed|date=November 2024}}}}. In March 1859 ({{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|WD=Q1327259|lt=Ansei era|1=Ansei}} 6), a lantern was added to the top of the guidepost by pilgrims from Imafuku village heading to Mount Ōmine. The guidepost reads:
"Left: Osaka Road. Right: Nozaki, Nara, Ise Road. Tōsonzan Pilgrimage Group."
Until the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}} period, a sacred old enoki tree, over 1,000 years old, stood on the shrine grounds{{Sfn|Honda|1913|p={{page needed|date=November 2024}}}}.
The Imafuku Ebisu Shrine on the premises is known as "Imafuku Ebisu". The annual Ebisu Market held every January has continued since before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}<ref name="Osaka City" />.
The shrine grounds also host the {{ill|Kojirō Inari Shrine|ja|小女郎稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojirō Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 皇大神宮 (大阪市)}}, which is mentioned in Volume 3 of the '''{{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsu Meisho Zue|1=Settsu Meisho Zue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498549}}'''. This Inari shrine was originally located on {{ill|Kitsune-yama|ja|狐山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsune-yama|1=Q119380142|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q119380142}} in {{ill|Namazue Village|ja|鯰江村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namazue Village|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鯰江町|1=Namazue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48519631}}. It is considered one of the "Three Great Inari Shrines of Kansai", alongside {{ill|Genkurō Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkurō Inari Shrine|1=Genkurō Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563977}} and {{ill|Shindōmori Kuzunoha Inari Shrine|ja|信太森葛葉稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindōmori Kuzunoha Inari Shrine|1=Shinodanomori Kuzunoha Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386029}}<ref name="Osaka City" />.

{{Shrine | Name = Kotai Jingū (皇大神宮) | image = {{image requested|cat=Osaka city}} | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}} {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka City|1=Osaka}} {{ill|Jōtō-ku, Osaka|en|Jōtō-ku, Osaka|qid=Q490508|lt=Joto Ward|1=Jōtō-ku, Osaka}} {{ill|Imafuku Minami|ja|今福南|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imafuku Minami|1=Imafuku-minami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q76307293}} 2-chome 12-31 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 41 | Latitude seconds = 51.7 | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 33 | Longitude seconds = 3.1 | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大御神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Ōmikami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Former Village Shrine|ja|旧村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former Village Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} | Founded = Unknown | Main hall = | Alias = {{Ublist| Imafuku Ebisu | Imafuku Kotai Jingū }} | Annual festival = {{Ublist| {{ill|April 22|en|April 22|qid=Q2526|lt=April 22|1=April 22}} | {{ill|October 22|en|October 22|qid=Q2957|lt=October 22|1=October 22}} }} | Map = Japan Osaka city }}
'''Kotai Jingū''' (皇大神宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Imafuku Minami|ja|今福南|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imafuku Minami|1=Imafuku-minami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q76307293}}, {{ill|Jōtō-ku, Osaka|en|Jōtō-ku, Osaka|qid=Q490508|lt=Joto Ward|1=Jōtō-ku, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka City|1=Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is affectionately known as "Imafuku Ebisu".
== History ==
The founding year is unknown<ref name="Osaka City">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/joto/page/0000000740.html |title=皇大神宮 |publisher=Osaka City |accessdate=2024-08-15 |language=ja}}</ref>. It is said to have been established during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} when {{ill|Kuki Imafuku Mikuriya|ja|久岐今福御厨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki Imafuku Mikuriya|qq=}} was developed<ref name="Osaka City" />. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} edict, it was known as the {{ill|Thirty Guardian Deities Shrine|ja|三十番神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thirty Guardian Deities Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Under the modern shrine Ranking system, it was designated a village shrine, received accounting designation in 1911 ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 44), and became a {{ill|Shinsen heihaku ryō kyōshinsha|ja|神饌幣帛供進社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen heihaku ryō kyōshinsha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 1912. In 1947 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 22), it was removed from state control under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|qid=Q3029647|lt=Shintō Directive|1=Shinto Directive}}<ref name="Official">{{Cite web |url=https://koutaijingu.wixsite.com/mysite/blank-1 |title=由緒 |publisher=皇大神宮 |accessdate=2024-11-20 |language=ja}}</ref>.
The current shrine building was renovated in 1742 ({{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|qid=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}} 2), 1842 ({{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|qid=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō era|1=Tenpō}} 13), and 1880 (Meiji 13), and reconstructed by {{ill|Furukawa Shigeharu|ja|古川重春|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furukawa Shigeharu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who designed {{ill|Osaka Castle|ja|大阪城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂城|1=Osaka Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q321242}}, in 1932 (Shōwa 7)<ref name="Osaka City" /><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.osaka-jinjacho.jp/funai_jinja/dai4shibu/joto-ku/04006kotaijingu.html |title=皇大神宮 |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2024-08-15}}</ref>.
In the {{ill|Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|ja|阪神・淡路大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|1=Damage by Great Hanshin earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11392882}} of 1995 (Heisei 7), part of the worship hall collapsed. It was repaired the following year with a copper roof replacing the previous tile roof.
The {{ill|Kinoshita Yōemon Land Survey Record|ja|木下与右衛門御検地帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinoshita Yōemon Land Survey Record|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1594, Bunroku 3) states that the shrine was exempted from taxation on an area measuring 26 ken east to west and 17 ken north to south (1 tan, 4 se, 4 chōbu)<ref name="Official" />{{Sfn|Kimura|1971|p={{page needed|date=November 2024}}}}.
The stone guidepost at the southwest corner of the worship hall was erected in March 1833 (Tenpō 3) by six caretakers. Originally, it stood at the southeastern corner of the crossroads on the {{ill|Kotsutsumi Kaidō (Old Embankment Road)|ja|古堤街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotsutsumi Kaidō (Old Embankment Road)|1=Q11411014|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11411014}} (Yamato Road), a major route for pilgrimages to {{ill|Nozaki|ja|野崎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozaki|1=Q11645363|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11645363}}, {{ill|Mount Ōmine|en|Mount Ōmine|qid=Q3695989|lt=Ōmine Mountain|1=Mount Ōmine}}, and {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. A local legend recounts that villagers danced wildly here during the {{ill|Okagemairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okagemairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11263449}} pilgrimage frenzy, and the guidepost was later moved to the shrine grounds{{Sfn|Kimura|1971|p={{page needed|date=November 2024}}}}. In March 1859 ({{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|qid=Q1327259|lt=Ansei era|1=Ansei}} 6), a lantern was added to the top of the guidepost by pilgrims from Imafuku village heading to Mount Ōmine. The guidepost reads:
"Left: Osaka Road. Right: Nozaki, Nara, Ise Road. Tōsonzan Pilgrimage Group."
Until the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|qid=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}} period, a sacred old enoki tree, over 1,000 years old, stood on the shrine grounds{{Sfn|Honda|1913|p={{page needed|date=November 2024}}}}.
The Imafuku Ebisu Shrine on the premises is known as "Imafuku Ebisu". The annual Ebisu Market held every January has continued since before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|qid=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}<ref name="Osaka City" />.
The shrine grounds also host the {{ill|Kojirō Inari Shrine|ja|小女郎稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojirō Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 皇大神宮 (大阪市)}}, which is mentioned in Volume 3 of the '''{{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsu Meisho Zue|1=Settsu Meisho Zue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11498549}}'''. This Inari shrine was originally located on {{ill|Kitsune-yama|ja|狐山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsune-yama|1=Q119380142|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q119380142}} in {{ill|Namazue Village|ja|鯰江村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namazue Village|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鯰江町|1=Namazue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q48519631}}. It is considered one of the "Three Great Inari Shrines of Kansai", alongside {{ill|Genkurō Inari Shrine|ja|源九郎稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkurō Inari Shrine|1=Genkurō Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11563977}} and {{ill|Shindōmori Kuzunoha Inari Shrine|ja|信太森葛葉稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindōmori Kuzunoha Inari Shrine|1=Shinodanomori Kuzunoha Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11386029}}<ref name="Osaka City" />.
